title: The GPU Memory Hierarchy: CGMA Ratio, Coalescing & Shared-Memory Tiling

learningObjectives.description: Compute the CGMA ratio for a given kernel and use the Roofline model to determine whether it is compute-bound or memory-bound, identifying the ridge point for a target GPU
Explain memory coalescing requirements for global memory access and identify which access patterns (stride-1, stride-2, scattered) result in single vs. multiple transactions
Apply shared-memory tiling to a matrix multiply kernel, derive the improved CGMA ratio as a function of tile width T, and state the theoretical speedup relative to the naïve implementation
Identify where the 3DGS rasterizer sits on the Roofline model and explain which hardware resource (memory bandwidth vs. compute) limits its throughput

content:
## Memory Is the Bottleneck

After correctly mapping threads to data (Reading 1), the next question is how fast those threads can actually run. For most real kernels the answer is determined not by the arithmetic units but by the **memory subsystem**: how quickly can operands be delivered to the CUDA cores? This reading develops the key quantitative framework — the CGMA ratio and the roofline model — and shows exactly how it applies to the 3DGS rasterizer.

## The GPU Memory Hierarchy

CUDA exposes five distinct memory spaces with radically different latency and bandwidth:

| Memory Type | Scope | Location | Latency | Size (per SM) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Registers | Per thread | On-chip | ≈1 cycle | ≈64K 32-bit regs |
| Shared memory | Per block | On-chip | ≈5–30 cycles | 48–100 KB |
| L1 cache | Per SM | On-chip | ≈30–40 cycles | 32–128 KB |
| L2 cache | Per device | On-chip | ≈200 cycles | 4–50 MB |
| Global memory (DRAM/HBM) | Per device | Off-chip | ≈500–800 cycles | GBs |
| Constant memory | Per device | Off-chip, cached | ≈5 (hit) / ≈500 (miss) | 64 KB |

In CUDA source code, memory type is controlled by variable declaration:

```cuda
__global__ void kernel(float* d_data) {
    float reg_var;                   // register (per-thread, on-chip)
    __shared__ float s_tile[16][16]; // shared memory (per-block, on-chip)
    // d_data is in global memory (off-chip)
    __constant__ float c_val;        // constant memory (read-only, cached)
}
```

The enormous latency gap between registers/shared memory (≈1–30 cycles) and global memory (≈500–800 cycles) is the fundamental challenge of GPU programming. A kernel that frequently stalls waiting for global memory reads is **memory-bound** — it wastes the compute capacity of the ALUs.

## The CGMA Ratio: Quantifying the Bottleneck

Kirk & Hwu define the **Compute-to-Global-Memory-Access (CGMA) ratio** as the number of floating-point operations performed per byte of global memory accessed:

$$\text{CGMA} = \frac{\text{Floating-point operations}}{\text{Global memory accesses (bytes)}}$$

This single number determines whether a kernel is compute-bound or memory-bound on a given GPU.

### Case Study: Naïve Matrix Multiply

For an \(N \times N\) matrix multiplication \(P = M \times N\), each output element \(P[i][j]\) requires:
- \(N\) multiply-add operations = \(2N\) floating-point operations
- \(2N\) reads from global memory: one element from row \(i\) of \(M\) and one from column \(j\) of \(N\)

With 4 bytes per float32:

$$\text{CGMA}_{\text{naive}} = \frac{2N \text{ FP ops}}{2N \times 4 \text{ bytes}} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25 \text{ FLOPs/byte}$$

The maximum achievable throughput is then:

$$\text{Achievable FLOP/s} = \min\left(\text{Peak FLOP/s},\ \text{Bandwidth} \times \text{CGMA}\right)$$

On the NVIDIA G80 (the architecture analyzed throughout Kirk & Hwu):
- Peak FP32 throughput: **367 GFlop/s**
- Global memory bandwidth: **86.4 GB/s**
- Achievable at CGMA = 0.25: \(86.4 \times 0.25 = 21.6\) GFlop/s

The naïve matrix multiply achieves only **5.9%** of the G80's peak compute capability, purely because of memory bandwidth limitation.

This result is not specific to the G80. On a modern NVIDIA A100:
- Peak FP32 throughput: **19.5 TFlop/s**
- HBM2e bandwidth: **2.0 TB/s**
- Achievable at CGMA = 0.25: \(2.0 \times 10^3 \times 0.25 = 500\) GFlop/s = **2.6%** of peak

Despite 23× more bandwidth than the G80, the A100 is *more* compute-bound at low CGMA ratios because its arithmetic throughput grew even faster than its bandwidth. The memory wall intensifies with each GPU generation.

## Memory Coalescing

Global memory bandwidth is not simply a fixed pipeline — its effective throughput depends critically on the **access pattern** of the 32 threads in a warp.

**The coalescing rule**: when all 32 threads in a warp access a contiguous, naturally aligned block of memory in a single instruction, the hardware coalesces those 32 accesses into **one memory transaction**. When the accesses are scattered, each may require a separate transaction.

| Access pattern | Transactions | Effective bandwidth |
|---|---|---|
| Stride-1 (consecutive), aligned | 1 | 100% |
| Stride-2 (every other address) | 2 | 50% |
| Stride-4 | 4 | 25% |
| Random (32 distinct cache lines) | up to 32 | ≈3% |

The practical implication: **array-of-structures (AoS) layouts are often uncoalesced; structure-of-arrays (SoA) layouts coalesce naturally**.

Example — suppose each Gaussian has `{float x, y, z, opacity}` and we store them as AoS:
```
Gaussian[0].x, Gaussian[0].y, Gaussian[0].z, Gaussian[0].opacity,
Gaussian[1].x, Gaussian[1].y, Gaussian[1].z, Gaussian[1].opacity,
...
```
When thread `k` reads `Gaussian[k].x`, consecutive threads read addresses that are 16 bytes apart (the stride of one full struct), not 4 bytes apart. Each read requires a separate cache line. With 32 threads: up to 32 transactions instead of 1.

With SoA layout:
```
all_x[0], all_x[1], ..., all_x[N-1],
all_y[0], all_y[1], ...
```
Thread `k` reading `all_x[k]` accesses addresses spaced 4 bytes apart — stride-1, perfectly coalesced.

In the 3DGS training pipeline, Gaussian attributes (positions, colors, scales, rotations, opacities) are stored as separate contiguous tensors (SoA), which is why the CUDA kernels achieve reasonable bandwidth utilization.

## Shared-Memory Tiling: Multiplying the CGMA Ratio

The solution to the CGMA problem is **tiling with shared memory**: load data from slow global memory into fast shared memory once, then let all threads in the block reuse it.

### Tiled Matrix Multiply

Divide the matrices into \(T \times T\) tiles. Each thread block loads one tile of \(M\) and one tile of \(N\) into shared memory cooperatively (one element per thread), computes partial dot products using the shared-memory data, then advances to the next tile:

```cuda
__shared__ float M_tile[TILE_WIDTH][TILE_WIDTH];
__shared__ float N_tile[TILE_WIDTH][TILE_WIDTH];

for (int phase = 0; phase < N / TILE_WIDTH; phase++) {
    // Cooperative load: each thread loads one element
    M_tile[ty][tx] = M[row * N + (phase * TILE_WIDTH + tx)];
    N_tile[ty][tx] = N[(phase * TILE_WIDTH + ty) * N + col];
    __syncthreads();

    // Compute partial dot product from shared memory (fast!)
    for (int k = 0; k < TILE_WIDTH; k++)
        Pvalue += M_tile[ty][k] * N_tile[k][tx];
    __syncthreads();
}
```

With tile width \(T\):
- Each element is loaded from global memory **once** and used **T times**
- CGMA increases to:

$$\text{CGMA}_{\text{tiled}} = T \times \text{CGMA}_{\text{naive}} = \frac{T}{4}$$

For \(T = 16\): CGMA = 4.0. For \(T = 32\): CGMA = 8.0.

On the G80 with \(T = 16\): achievable throughput rises from 21.6 GFlop/s to \(86.4 \times 4.0 = 345.6\) GFlop/s — **94% of peak** instead of 5.9%. Shared-memory tiling is the single most impactful optimization for memory-bound kernels.

The 3DGS rasterizer applies exactly this principle: rather than each pixel thread independently fetching each Gaussian's attributes from global memory, the block cooperatively loads a batch of Gaussians into `__shared__` arrays, then all 256 pixel threads iterate over the shared batch. This is the `BLOCK_SIZE = 16` loading loop in `forward.cu`.

## The Roofline Model

The roofline model (Williams et al., 2009) provides a visual framework for understanding performance limits. For a given GPU:

- **Peak compute ceiling**: \(\pi\) FLOP/s (e.g., 19.5 TFlop/s for A100 FP32)
- **Memory bandwidth ceiling**: \(\beta \times I\) where \(\beta\) is bandwidth (GB/s) and \(I\) is the **arithmetic intensity** (= CGMA) in FLOP/byte

The achievable performance \(P\) for a kernel with arithmetic intensity \(I\):

$$P = \min(\pi,\ \beta \times I)$$

The **ridge point** is the arithmetic intensity at which the two ceilings meet:

$$I^* = \frac{\pi}{\beta}$$

For the A100: \(I^* = 19,500 / 2,000 = 9.75\) FLOP/byte. Kernels with \(I < 9.75\) are **memory-bound** — bounded by bandwidth. Kernels with \(I > 9.75\) are **compute-bound** — bounded by ALU throughput.

| GPU | Peak FP32 (TFlop/s) | Bandwidth (TB/s) | Ridge point (FLOP/byte) |
|---|---|---|---|
| NVIDIA G80 (2006) | 0.367 | 0.086 | 4.3 |
| NVIDIA V100 (2017) | 14.0 | 0.900 | 15.6 |
| NVIDIA A100 (2020) | 19.5 | 2.000 | 9.75 |
| NVIDIA H100 (2022) | 51.2 | 3.350 | 15.3 |

### Where Does the 3DGS Rasterizer Sit?

A rough estimate for the 3DGS alpha-blending inner loop:
- Per Gaussian per pixel: ≈20 FP ops (2D Gaussian evaluation, alpha computation, color accumulation)
- Per Gaussian per pixel: ≈40 bytes read from shared memory (position, cov2D, color, opacity)
- Arithmetic intensity ≈ 20/40 = **0.5 FLOP/byte**

On the A100, with \(I = 0.5 < I^* = 9.75\), the rasterizer inner loop is **deeply memory-bound** even when using shared memory. Optimization strategies must focus on increasing data reuse or reducing the number of Gaussians processed per tile — not on arithmetic throughput.

## Warp Occupancy and Latency Hiding

High occupancy (many active warps per SM) is the GPU's primary strategy for hiding memory latency. While one warp waits 500 cycles for a global memory load, the SM's warp scheduler switches to another warp that has its operands ready.

**Theoretical occupancy** is limited by three resources per SM:
1. **Registers**: if a kernel uses \(r\) registers per thread and the SM has \(R_{\max}\) total registers, at most \(\lfloor R_{\max} / (r \times B) \rfloor\) blocks of size \(B\) fit simultaneously, where \(\lfloor \cdot \rfloor\) denotes floor to the nearest integer multiple of the warp size.
2. **Shared memory**: if a kernel uses \(s\) bytes of shared memory per block and the SM has \(S_{\max}\) bytes, at most \(\lfloor S_{\max} / s \rfloor\) blocks fit.
3. **Block count limit**: hardware caps the number of concurrent blocks per SM (typically 32).

Achieved occupancy = (actual active warps) / (maximum warps per SM). The NVIDIA Occupancy Calculator or `ncu --metrics sm__warps_active.avg.pct_of_peak_sustained_active` provides the measured value.

A key insight: **100% occupancy is not always necessary**. If memory latency is fully hidden with 50% occupancy, increasing to 100% provides no throughput gain — and the extra register pressure required might force register spillage to local memory, making things worse. The optimization goal is *sufficient* occupancy to hide the dominant latency, not maximum possible occupancy.

references.label: Kirk & Hwu 2010
references.title: Programming Massively Parallel Processors — Chapter 5: CUDA Memories, Chapter 6: Performance Considerations

references.label: Williams et al. 2009
references.title: Roofline: An Insightful Visual Performance Model for Floating-Point Programs and Multicore Architectures
